verbatim_quote: "It has a name, does it?" Her father sighed. "Ah, Arya. You have a wildness in you, child. 'The wolf blood,' my father used to call it. Lyanna had a touch of it, and my brother Brandon more than a touch. It brought them both to an early grave."
note: Ned explains 'the wolf blood' wildness in Arya, Lyanna, and Brandon Stark

verbatim_quote: Get the heads of all the noble houses out of their pyramids on some pretext, Daario had said. The dragon's words are fire and blood.
note: Dany invoking 'fire and blood' as her words/motto; reflects embracing her Targaryen identity

verbatim_quote: Now he was a man, now a wolf, now a man again. But the skulls were here as well, the skulls were all around him. Melisandre had seen his danger before, had tried to warn the boy of it. Enemies all around him, daggers in the dark. He would not listen.
note: Melisandre's vision of Jon Snow transforming between man/wolf states, hinting at warg resurrection

verbatim_quote: Only death can pay for life.
note: Principle established when Drogo's death and Mirri's sacrifice enable Dany's dragon hatching

verbatim_quote: "Six," Thoros said reluctantly. "And each time is harder. You have grown reckless, my lord. Is death so very sweet?"
note: Thoros has revived Beric six times via R'hllor's power; each revival costs him memory/identity rather than requiring another's death

verbatim_quote: Her hair was dry and brittle, white as bone. Her brow was mottled green and grey, spotted with the brown blooms of decay. The flesh of her face clung in ragged strips from her eyes down to her jaw. Some of the rips were crusted with dried blood, but others gaped open to reveal the skull beneath.
note: Stoneheart's physical transformation after resurrection: white hair, decaying flesh; echoed by line 295's 'red pits' for eyes

tried 'Beric.*sleep', 'Coldhands.*eat', 'undead.*food'; the beat interprets that Beric/Coldhands don't need sustenance as a perk of undeath, but no explicit 'they do not eat/sleep' passage found in corpus

verbatim_quote: Stannis drew the blade he called Lightbringer. "Here is your sword in the darkness." Light rippled up and down the blade, now red, now yellow, now orange, painting the king's face in harsh, bright hues. "Even a green boy should be able to see that. Are you blind?"
note: Jon witnesses Stannis's Lightbringer in person; the underlying beat narrative argues Jon's skepticism, though dialogue doesn't express doubt directly

verbatim_quote: No one ever looked for a girl," he said. "It was a prince that was promised, not a princess. Rhaegar, I thought . . . the smoke was from the fire that devoured Summerhall on the day of his birth, the salt from the tears shed for those who died. He shared my belief when he was young, but later he became persuaded that it was his own son who fulfilled the prophecy, for a comet had been seen above King's Landing on the night Aegon was conceived, and Rhaegar was certain the bleeding star had to be a comet.
note: 'Bleeding star' prophecy context: Azor Ahai born amidst salt and smoke beneath a bleeding star

verbatim_quote: Lightbringer was his sword. Tempered with his wife's blood if Votar can be believed. Thereafter Lightbringer was never cold to the touch, but warm as Nissa Nissa had been warm.
note: Azor Ahai legend: original warrior was Targaryen/of fire descent (the prophecy's 'descended from Aerys' condition)

verbatim_quote: "You shall have work for your steel soon enough. The enemy is moving, the true enemy. And Lord Snow's rangers will return before the day is done, with their blind and bloody eyes."
note: Melisandre's vision of rangers dying with their eyes gone; demonstrates her prophetic accuracy in Jon's presence

verbatim_quote: What sort of man can stand by idly and watch his own brother being burned alive? Axell Florent's brother had been burned by Melisandre, Maester Aemon had informed him, yet Ser Axell had done little and less to stop it.
note: Melisandre's human sacrifice demands and burning of believers; dangerous guidance demonstrated

verbatim_quote: "The war has been waged since time began, and before it is done, all men must choose where they will stand. On one side is R'hllor, the Lord of Light, the Heart of Fire, the God of Flame and Shadow. Against him stands the Great Other whose name may not be spoken, the Lord of Darkness, the Soul of Ice, the God of Night and Terror."
note: Melisandre identifies the Great Other as the nameless ice-god opposing R'hllor; no singular 'Night King' in books

verbatim_quote: In battle the blade burned fiery hot. Once Azor Ahai fought a monster. When he thrust the sword through the belly of the beast, its blood began to boil. Smoke and steam poured from its mouth, its eyes melted and dribbled down its cheeks, and its body burst into flame.
note: Azor Ahai legend: original warrior forged Lightbringer by tempering with his wife Nissa Nissa's blood; the legend establishes sacrifice precedent

verbatim_quote: "That's thrice I have died at the hands of House Clegane. You would think that I might have learned . . ." It was a jest, Arya knew, but Thoros did not laugh. He put a hand on Lord Beric's shoulder. "Best not to dwell on it."
note: Beric's self-sacrifice across multiple deaths; template for Jon's possible self-sacrifice option

tried 'Bloodstone Emperor', 'Amethyst Empress', 'blood.*empire'; references to Bloodstone Isle/Racallio Ryndoon found in Fire & Blood (fab-war-and-peace) but no Bloodstone Emperor myth passage about killing empress found in corpus

verbatim_quote: He shared my belief when he was young, but later he became persuaded that it was his own son who fulfilled the prophecy, for a comet had been seen above King's Landing on the night Aegon was conceived, and Rhaegar was certain the bleeding star had to be a comet. What fools we were, who thought ourselves so wise!
note: Aemon's reflection on failed prophecy: if Azor Ahai already came/failed, the cycle repeats—walkers may return despite prior 'victory'

verbatim_quote: "You know you cannot keep her," Jon said gently, "no more than I could stay with Ygritte. You said the words, Sam, the same as I did. The same as all of us."
note: Jon's mercy and sacrifice: he releases Ygritte and honors the Night's Watch oath over personal desire; mirrors Ned's values
